item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really unique and elegant in its simplicity. Committing to explaining the weird absurdities of the situation as tightly as you have makes this read very smoothly, and is basically the perfect length to introduce your doomsday scenario will still maintaining interest in the reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, so this is a supernatural mystery. Comes off as Series I, I like it. +1. The meaning didn't jump out at me immediately which my short attention span interpreted as Bad, but after making an effort to reread was worth it.

Initial thoughts:
+ show block
– hide block

Looks cool, but can't get over the first paragraph of the description.

Description: SCP-3406 is a ritual that causes sapient entities with a physical form to become capable of locomotion. Entities that already possess this ability, such as humans, will remain unaffected — anomalous effects are most pronounced in entities with inanimate bodies, or ones that are "bound" to inanimate objects.

Like a ghost sealed to an object or something? A golem?

specific series of phrases from the as-of-yet untranslated █████████ language

Minor nitpick, I'd say just "specific series of phrases from an as-of-yet untranslated language", because how would you know what a language is called if it's untranslated?

The █████████ incantations are a very comprehensive and powerful symbol for Life.

…Never mind then.

item.title: Admin Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
Do not post about any spam PMs you get from people telling you to check out articles and vote on them. Staff is aware.

This is a closed staff post. Do not respond for any reason if you are not staff.

item.title: Re: How can you see into my eyes, like open doors?
item.content:encoded: You should know that another user, QuitStalin, is also doing this now, and is not accepting PMs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the same problem I have with this person. They sent me a PM from an account called QuitStalin, so there's a third username in the mix here. When I tried to email them back to let them know that their image was broken (seems like a real easy way to get downvoted) the client gave me a notification that they're not accepting PMs at this time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. If you're going to send a PM to someone asking them to vote, make sure to turn your PMs on so that I don't have to resort to this.
  2. Your image file is broken and needs to be fixed.

item.title: More PM's
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry Magnus for adding to the me too pile but another different user mailed me named QuitStalin. They have been a wikidot user for only an hour at the time of this post. He said,
"Please vote on this, it's really good: http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-3406"
I'd thought I'd add this as it was a different user.

item.title: Re: How can you see into my eyes, like open doors?
item.content:encoded: Ooooooh, that explains things. Sorry you had that happen to you. I wish you the best of luck! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having had a while to think about this, I think this article is built on what is essentially fridge horror. I've seen a lot of complaints about the sapience thing, but it all comes down to something subtle:

+ show block
– hide block

These objects were always sapient, we just couldn't tell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall, I like it. However, there are some problems…

+Critique
-I hope that helps...

I did notice one glaring discrepancy:

SCP-3406 is a ritual that causes sapient entities with a physical form to become capable of locomotion.

The article goes on to mention SCP-3406-1 instances that have no sapience whatsoever, such as vehicles, and a stainless steel Leatherman-brand pocket tool. To make matters more confusing, vehicles are already capable of locomotion, and this fact is pointed out in the article. I think you meant to say something closer to:

SCP-3406 is a ritual that causes physical entities to become capable of independent locomotion.

Somewhere in there you should also add that the ritual also causes entities, physical or otherwise, to gain sapience, as long as they are somehow physically located in the ritual's range of effect.

I don't mean to nitpick, but the history and notice are both technically addenda, so they should be labeled as such. You can have multiple addenda as long as they're distinguished from each other. You can just number them, or give the date when they're added to the document. Just look at how the other writers do it. There are good articles with a section labeled 'History', but those are from the early days of this site.

Finally, a containment breach by an outside party usually shouldn't change the object's classification from Safe to Keter. Safe means that if you leave the object alone, it will stay in containment and won't do anything. By this definition, even a weapon is Safe. Keter means that the anomaly is difficult to contain. At best, the Foundation must resort to costly and complex containment methods. At worst, the Foundation can only hope that they're actually containing the anomaly. Your anomaly is a ritual. It is a tool; it can be good or bad depending on how it is used. However, it does nothing if left alone, so it is Safe. A containment breach caused by Groups of Interest does not make the ritual itself any more dangerous than it was before, and any misuse of that ritual says more about the Groups of Interest than the ritual.

Other than that, looks all good to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read this and didn't understand how it could only work on "sapient" entities, and yet the image is of a pocket knife. Looking in the discussion page for your explanation was pretty underwhelming and seems out of character for the Foundation. Why would they take the objects' claims of pre-existing sapience at face value? Do they keep a log of objects experimented on, and if so, have any been shown to be incapable of this transformation? If the answer to either is "no", then the assumption that the objects were already sapient makes no sense.

Sorry, but it's not working for me. -1 as it's presented right now.

item.title: Not bad.
item.content:encoded: I like how it's tied to a larger story. The article by itself is good as well +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This wouldn't work save for the Misery Makes Company addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dig it. I hope the fiasco with whoever that cold-mailer is doesn't mess things up too badly because he mailed me too. I don't blame him, whatever his intentions, since I probably wouldn't have seen this article anytime soon without it. Anyway, I particularly like how the POI who wrote the notes tried to work through the how and why of the process in a somewhat scientific manner. Presumably the Foundation will be as or more thorough in exploring what makes the process happen, rather than just leaving it at "it's magic," and calling it a day. If I have one gripe, it's the same one I always have, and that's the expungement of data when only a redaction is required, although in this case it only happened once, and it could have been a direct order by a member of the O-5 council because they actually decided that nobody should know what was written there aside from them. But if that's the case, there is no indication as to what that data might actually be.

item.title: MOD POST - INFO - CLOSED
item.content:encoded:
Staff are aware of the issue.

Please stop adding "me too!" posts on this thread, it's cluttering up the forums, and is not productive to the discussion.

Edit: Additionally, please do not speculate on potential rule infractions. Rumors and accusations are bad form.

Thank you.

THIS IS A CLOSED STAFF POST, PLEASE DO NOT RESPOND UNLESS YOU ARE STAFF

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given the new account, username and rather ham-fisted way this was carried out, it wouldn't surprise me if this was some random trying to grief CWW.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe that's what this person is going for. I got the message too, thought it was really odd. Wonder if it is really a friend. Does the author know this is happening?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got the PM too. Definitely suspicious that this is another mass PM thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I barely post and I got this PM. No idea who this person is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [E: I tend to believe this whole thing is a frame-up]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just noting that I got one too, and I haven’t been active on site for about a month now, so if I had to guess I’d say it’s not just a random new guy.

Decided to withold my vote until this is sorted out, since it’s a contest entry n’ all. TumblrMarx, if you’re real and end up reading this, you should probably stop. Two users were disqualified from the last contest for sending out spam/solicitation PMs to garner votes, and this attention might end up harming Cdub’s article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this kind of PM marketing against the rules? I kind of feel like that would be a rule, but haven't seen it anywhere.

I got it too, btw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That name is setting off alarms for me tbh. I don't mean to be starting something here, but I think this could be a sockpuppet or something. Especially considering that TumblrMarx isn't even on the site and has no comments or edits. Not even a sandbox.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got it too.

It was from TumblrMarx. This article was written by Communism will win. Now, TumblrMarx did say it was a friend of theirs, and they're a new member, so I'll believe it.

But the fact remains that an article that was written by CWW two weeks ago will most definitely have been read a whole bunch. I have no reason to believe the article is not being read. I know for a fact I did read it two weeks ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got the same PM. Considering this is a Doomcon entry with a good amount of votes I'm not sure what's going on there.

Anyway, I agree with what you said too. Without the accompying pieces this article is rather plain, and almost unnecessary with the pieces imo. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some guy PM'ed me a link to this article saying that "not many have read it."

Based on these votes, it's safe to assume a good amount of people have read it.

All in all, the whole thing didn't really interest me.

Novoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea behind it, and the execution was beginning to take shape. However, I feel like it could have had a bit more of a punch at the end, right now is kind of underwhelming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I reverted an edit that incompletely tagged this. If I am in error, Staff, I apologize and won't object to my own "correction" being corrected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm changing my downvote to an upvote after reading more into the rest of the canon/storyline/entry.

The parts fit together nicely, and I don't think a puzzle should be judged only by some of its pieces.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll make sure to change it after the contest is over. I'd do it now, but rules and such.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love the funny/weird/terrifying concept and stories but I think you should change "locomotion" to "independent locomotion" since in your first tale a car comes to life except it was already capable of movement, except now it can do it by itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me of your one skip about things that randomly turn into spiders. Maybe they're related?

Anyways, nice work. Entertaining throughout, and I felt the "Oh, shit!" moment at the end.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, wow, okay, now I get it, shit.

The first time I saw this, I kind of blipped over it. And that meant the rest of the entries didn't make a whole lot of sense, at least in terms of connectivity.

It is also worth noting, I kind of forgot there was a contest going on until, like, right now, so… Yeah, oops. c.c;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's meant primarily as a setup for the rest of my team's entries. But next time, I'll be sure to write my article to your specifications.

item.title: Re: Uncommon Doom
item.content:encoded:
I hate to be pedantic, but most doomsdays aren't gonna happen more than, like, twice, so I dunno how 'common' they can be.

Nitpicks aside, I do understand what you mean by that. I imagine that the Foundation writes up as many potential doomsday scenarios as they can think of apocalyptic scenarios that might require a pre-planned response, even if they only have one anomaly that could do the thing in question.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
EDIT: Zyn is right it should be in Policy - I was typing as this was being posted

Yeah, no. Notice it says "attack", not critique.
If someone had said "This was meh. Scantron, you're an idiot.", you would have a leg to stand on.

Saying "this is meh and furthermore it's underwhelming by your standards" is a legitimate criticism of just about any work, anywhere, since there's a world of a difference between someone doing their best and it just about getting there with glaring faults and a veteran simply doing shoddy work, even if the resulting pieces are comparable.

Saying otherwise would be both dishonest and unhelpful - and the last I caught, the criticism policy isn't aimed at shielding anyone from uh, actual criticism of their work however harsh it need be.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I don't see "I expected more" as a personal attack, but I tend to also be more positive with regards to viewing responses, so I append "…because I usually enjoy this author's work/see good things from them, but this didn't happen to be the case here".

That said, this discussion should take place in the Policy subforum, so that will be the last post from me on the topic in this discussion.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
This probably isn't the place for this discussion, but we can have it here if you'd like.

"Critique the work, don't attack the writer" is Rule #1 of the criticism policy. Saying "I expected more from this writer" is an attack on the writer, and violates the criticism policy. That policy doesn't state "unless it's a contest piece" or "unless it's a 001". Our rules specifically state to critique only the content produced, not who produced it.

Especially considering this is a contest piece, I'd argue we need to be more aware of this kind of infraction now than ever.

item.title: Uncommon Doom
item.content:encoded: I like the article, but I'm not convinced that a mass animation is a common enough doomsday to warrant its own classification. It seems like the event described is "anomalous information becomes common knowledge" doomsday. I'll need to compare it to the other entries.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Uh… I feel like this staff response is unnecessarily vehement with regards to the statement made.

The expectations you have for an author have nothing to do with the content of the article you're critiquing.

I'm not certain that this is always the case, particularly with things like 001 proposals.

Given that I'm currently preoccupied with other IRL things, I will need to address this in full later, but I don't think the strong staff response was warranted or particularly necessary here.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Just felt like adding that I was expecting a much heavier entry from Scantron.

Again, this is an unacceptable thing to say in critiquing an article. The expectations you have for an author have nothing to do with the content of the article you're critiquing.

Do not make this kind of comment again.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I was underwhelmed regardless. Just felt like adding that I was expecting a much heavier entry from Scantron.

This feels like too mundane of a doomsday to stand out.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Addressing the author when critiquing a work is unacceptable and is in violation of established criticism policy. Critique only the work being submitted - your expectations of the author don't have anything to do with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh. Underwhelmed. I was expecting more from scantron.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sold on the idea that the Foundation would take the animated entities' testimonies at face value. And if the Foundation has a way of somehow communicating with souls bonded to inanimate objects, I'd say that's notable enough to warrant mention in the article.

Overall I think this article has a good concept, and I like the implication that sapient-but-not-animate objects are all around us, but I can't shake the feeling that there's a lede being buried here. There's a clear indication of set-up, but not a lot of follow-through. Maybe that's a consequence of saving the good stuff for other entries, but it makes this harder to enjoy on its own. +-0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, thanks to 3406, you can just ask them. That or they have some limited/nontraditional way of expressing themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this affects things which are sapient, but not animate? How could the Foundation know if something was sapient before it was animated?

item.title: How can you see into my eyes, like open doors?
item.content:encoded:
This is my (first) entry into the Doomsday Contest for team "Bring Me To Life"! The image was created entirely by myself. Thanks to my team for brainstorming and critting, and especially thanks to Monkeysky for coming up with our Doomsday scenario.

See also:

  • Misery Makes Company
  • The Three Documents of "The Artist Formerly Known As Chuck"
  • UF-00031

EDIT:

I don't know who TumblrMarx is, I didn't ask them to do this for me and they're probably trying to get me disqualified, I'm sorry they bothered you with a PM, staff is aware of the situation and specifically closed discussion of it here. That should cover everything.
